topic.title: Standard 1: Understand Your Role
topic.summary: Understanding your main duties and responsibilities, working in agreed ways, building effective working relationships, and working in partnership with others.

questions.source_criterion: 1.1a Describe your main duties and responsibilities
questions.prompt: What is the main purpose of a job description in a care role?

options.label: It outlines your main duties, responsibilities, and who you report to
It lists every single task you will ever do in your role
It is only needed when you first apply for the job
It is a legal contract that cannot be changed

questions.explanation: A job description gives you an overall picture of your role, including your main duties and responsibilities and who you report to. It cannot list every task you will ever do, but it should give you a clear understanding of what is expected of you.

questions.source_criterion: 1.1b List the standards, codes of conduct and practices that relate to your role
questions.prompt: Which document sets out the moral and ethical standards expected of all health and social care workers in England?

options.label: The Code of Conduct for Healthcare Support Workers and Adult Social Care Workers in England
The Health and Safety at Work Act 1974
The individual's care plan
The organisation's staff rota

questions.explanation: The Code of Conduct for Healthcare Support Workers and Adult Social Care Workers in England sets out the moral and ethical standards expected of all workers. The Care Certificate itself is another key standard that new workers must complete before working without supervision.

questions.source_criterion: 1.1d Explain how your previous experiences, attitudes and beliefs may affect the way you work
questions.prompt: Why is it important for a care worker to be self-aware about their own attitudes and beliefs?

options.label: Because your attitudes and beliefs could lead you to make unfair assumptions about individuals if you are not aware of them
Because you should always impose your own beliefs on the individuals you support
Because having beliefs means you cannot be a good care worker
Because self-awareness is only relevant for managers, not care workers

questions.explanation: Your background, upbringing, and experiences shape your attitudes and beliefs. While these may have led you to choose care work, they could also cause you to make incorrect assumptions about others. Developing self-awareness helps you check this and work in a way that respects each individual's own beliefs and values.

questions.source_criterion: 1.2a Describe your employment rights and responsibilities
questions.prompt: Under the Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974, what right do you have as a care worker?

options.label: The right to work in a safe environment and to be provided with necessary safety equipment free of charge
The right to refuse any task you do not want to do
The right to decide your own working hours
The right to ignore safety procedures if you are busy

questions.explanation: The Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974 gives you the right to work in a safe environment and to be provided with the equipment you need to stay safe, free of charge. With those rights come responsibilities — you must also work in the ways your employer has told you are safe.

questions.source_criterion: 1.2b List the aims, objectives and values of the service in which they work
questions.prompt: Why is it important for you to understand your organisation's aims, objectives and values?

options.label: It helps you understand your own role and how your work contributes to what the organisation wants to achieve
It is only important for managers to know these
They are the same for every care organisation so there is no need to check
They only matter when the organisation is being inspected by the CQC

questions.explanation: Understanding your organisation's values, aims and objectives helps you see how your role fits into the bigger picture. If your employer does not have these written down, you should ask your manager to explain them to you.

questions.source_criterion: 1.2c Explain why it is important to work in ways that are agreed with their employer
questions.prompt: What could happen if you do not follow your employer's agreed ways of working?

options.label: You could harm yourself or others without meaning to, and could face disciplinary procedures
Nothing — agreed ways of working are just suggestions
You would only be given a verbal warning the first time
Your employer would simply change the agreed ways to match what you did

questions.explanation: Agreed ways of working ensure you are working within the law and providing care that meets individuals' needs. If you do not follow them, you could unintentionally harm yourself or others, and you could face disciplinary procedures, dismissal, or even prosecution.

questions.source_criterion: 1.2e Explain how and when to escalate any concerns they might have (whistleblowing)
questions.prompt: What should you do if you believe something is unsafe or illegal at work, but it involves your manager?

options.label: Follow your employer's whistleblowing procedure — speak to someone more senior or an external body such as the CQC
Ignore it because it involves your manager
Discuss it informally with other colleagues but take no formal action
Leave the job immediately without saying anything

questions.explanation: Whistleblowing means reporting things that are not right, illegal, or where someone is neglecting their duties. In most cases you start with your manager, but if the concern involves your manager, or if your concern is not acted on, you should follow your employer's whistleblowing procedure and report it to someone more senior or an external body.

questions.source_criterion: 1.2f Explain why it is important to be honest and identify where errors may have occurred and to tell the appropriate person
questions.prompt: When a mistake happens at work, why is it important to be honest and report it?

options.label: It allows action to be taken to reduce the impact, and lessons to be learned from what went wrong
Mistakes should be hidden to avoid getting into trouble
Only serious mistakes need to be reported
It is better to fix the mistake yourself without telling anyone

questions.explanation: Everyone makes mistakes. Being honest and identifying errors allows you to take action that may reduce the harm caused, and helps everyone learn from what went wrong. This is known as the Duty of Candour — being open and honest when things go wrong.

questions.source_criterion: 1.3a Describe their responsibilities to the individuals they support
questions.prompt: Which of the following is a key responsibility you have towards the individuals you support?

options.label: Ensuring their safety and welfare by following their care plan and involving them in decisions about their care
Making all decisions for them to save time
Only involving their family, never the individual themselves
Treating everyone the same regardless of their individual needs

questions.explanation: Your responsibilities include protecting individuals' safety and welfare, ensuring their care meets their needs by involving them in planning and review, and treating them fairly by promoting equality, diversity, and upholding their dignity and human rights.

questions.source_criterion: 1.3b Explain how a working relationship is different from a personal relationship
questions.prompt: What is the main difference between a working relationship and a personal relationship?

options.label: A working relationship is based on a professional role and agreed boundaries, while a personal relationship is based on friendship and choice
There is no difference — they are the same thing
A working relationship is always more important than a personal one
Personal relationships are allowed in a care setting but working relationships are not

questions.explanation: A working relationship is based around your professional role, with clear boundaries, policies and agreed ways of working. A personal relationship is based on friendship, choice and mutual interests. It is important to keep these separate to maintain professionalism and protect both you and the individuals you support.

questions.source_criterion: 1.3c Describe different working relationships in health and social care settings
questions.prompt: Which of the following are the four main types of working relationships in health and social care?

options.label: Individuals and their friends/family, colleagues and managers, people from other workplaces, and volunteers/community groups
Only doctors and nurses
Managers and external inspectors only
Care workers and the local authority only

questions.explanation: The four main working relationships are: individuals and their friends and family; your colleagues and managers; people from other workplaces (including advocates); and volunteers and community groups. All should involve mutual respect and a focus on working together in the individual's best interests.

questions.source_criterion: 1.4a & b Explain why it is important to work in teams and in partnership with others
questions.prompt: Why is partnership working important in health and social care?

options.label: Serious case reviews often show that failings in partnership working were a key factor in what went wrong
Partnership working is optional and only needed when there are problems
It is only necessary when working with other health professionals
Partnership working means doing everything yourself to avoid confusion

questions.explanation: Partnership working is essential — serious case reviews frequently identify failings in partnership working as a key factor when a vulnerable adult dies or comes to significant harm. Good communication, trust, and respect between everyone involved help provide better, safer care.

questions.source_criterion: 1.4d Demonstrate how and when to access support and advice about partnership working and resolving conflicts
questions.prompt: Where should you go for advice if a disagreement arises between workers from different agencies involved in an individual's care?

options.label: Speak to your manager or other experienced workers who have the skills and knowledge to advise you
Try to resolve it yourself without telling anyone
Stop working with the other agency altogether
Ignore the disagreement — it will resolve itself

questions.explanation: Unresolved conflict can affect the quality of care. You should ask for advice whenever you face a problem with partnership working. Your manager and other experienced workers are the right people to go to for guidance.

questions.source_criterion: 1.1 (new 2025) Identify different opportunities for career and professional development
questions.prompt: Which of the following is an example of a professional development opportunity for a care worker?

options.label: Undertaking a formal qualification such as an NVQ or apprenticeship
Only doing the minimum training required
Avoiding any additional training to focus on day-to-day tasks
Waiting for your manager to tell you what career to pursue

questions.explanation: Professional development includes qualifications, specialist training, apprenticeships, and other learning opportunities that help you grow in your role. Employers should invest in their workers' learning and development beyond the initial induction period.

questions.source_criterion: 1.1c Demonstrate that you are working in accordance with the agreed ways of working with your employer
questions.prompt: Where would you find the agreed ways of working for supporting a specific individual?

options.label: In the individual's care plan, which details how their care and support should be provided
By asking another individual you support what they think
They are not written down — you just use your best judgement
From a general internet search about care procedures

questions.explanation: Agreed ways of working with each individual are detailed in their care plan. These ensure you are working within the law and providing care that meets that person's specific needs. If you are unsure, you should always ask your manager.
